18: A type: 'Tie this scarlet cord in the window and bring your father and mother, your brothers and all your father's household into your house.' Rahab's scarlet cord has been typologically read as the blood of Christ marking the household for salvation — and the parallel is structurally exact: an outsider (a Gentile, a prostitute) is incorporated into God's covenant people by faith expressed through a sign (scarlet cord / blood); everyone inside the marked household is saved; everyone outside is not. Matthew's genealogy (Matthew 1:5) incorporates Rahab explicitly into the Messiah's lineage — the Gentile woman of faith is literally in the line of Christ. Rahab is one of the Bible's clearest illustrations that faith, not ethnicity, is the covenant criterion.
1: The dispatch of two spies to Jericho — and their lodging with Rahab — sets up one of the NT's most prominent OT examples of faith (Hebrews 11:31; James 2:25). But Rahab's ultimate significance is genealogical: Matthew's genealogy of Jesus places her directly in the line of the Messiah — 'Salmon the father of Boaz by Rahab' (Matthew 1:5). A Canaanite prostitute is in the bloodline of Jesus Christ. This is not incidental; the genealogy of Matthew is a theological statement about the kind of Messiah Jesus is — one who comes from the outcasts and Gentiles as well as from Israel's heroes, fulfilling God's purpose to bless all nations through Abraham's seed (Genesis 12:3).
2: The king of Jericho's intelligence report — 'men of Israel have come here tonight to search out the land' — is the first of many rejections and hostile searchings that prefigure Israel's (and ultimately humanity's) resistance to Christ. As Jericho's king sought to capture the spies who brought the word of YHWH's conquest, Herod sought to kill the infant Jesus who would accomplish the ultimate conquest (Matthew 2:13-16). The pattern of the powers of this age mobilizing against YHWH's agents of redemption runs through the whole Bible, from Pharaoh's infanticide to Herod's massacre — the adversary always knows that his kingdom is threatened and responds with rejection and force.
3: The king's demand — 'bring out the men who have come to you' — is Jericho's self-determined judgment: the city that rejected YHWH's messengers sealed its own doom. Jesus pronounces the same judgment pattern on cities that reject his messengers: 'And if anyone will not receive you or listen to your words, shake off the dust from your feet when you leave that house or town. Truly, I say to you, it will be more bearable on the day of judgment for the land of Sodom and Gomorrah than for that town' (Matthew 10:14-15). Jericho's rejection of the spies (YHWH's advance agents) anticipates the rejection that leads to judgment — but Rahab's reception creates an island of salvation within the doomed city.
4: Rahab's concealment of the spies and her deception of the king's messengers types the hidden nature of God's people in the midst of a world hostile to Christ. The church is 'hidden with Christ in God' (Colossians 3:3); the world does not know believers as it did not know Christ (1 John 3:1). Rahab's house was a place of secret salvation within a city of certain judgment — a picture of the church as the community of the saved dwelling within the world under divine sentence. The king's failure to find the spies types the powers' failure to extinguish the gospel: 'The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it' (John 1:5).
5: The closed gate of Jericho at nightfall types the closing of the age — the moment when no further entrance is possible. Jesus warns of this closure: 'Strive to enter through the narrow door. For many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able. When once the master of the house has risen and shut the door...' (Luke 13:24-25). Rahab's act of concealment happened before the gate closed — salvation is available within the time of opportunity, before the door shuts. The urgency of the gospel is this: Jericho's gate closes; the moment for safe passage is now.
6: The flax stalks under which the spies were hidden on Rahab's roof carry a hidden-life symbolism that resonates with the hiddenness of the risen Christ in the world. 'Your life is hidden with Christ in God' (Colossians 3:3) — believers are concealed under something ordinary (the flat roof, the mundane life of the world) while the enemy searches. The spies' survival depended entirely on Rahab's concealment — as believers' lives depend entirely on Christ's concealment of them in himself. The enemy cannot find what Christ has hidden.
7: The king's men pursuing the spies toward the Jordan ford types the futile pursuit of those whom Christ has freed. As Pharaoh's army pursued Israel to the Red Sea and was destroyed there (Exodus 14:28), Jericho's men pursued the spies toward the Jordan — the very water that would eventually destroy Jericho's power. The Jordan becomes the boundary that defines safety: those on YHWH's side of the Jordan are under his protection; those pursuing them are on the side of the condemned city. The pursuit ends at the crossing-point, which types baptism — those united to Christ through death and resurrection are beyond the reach of condemnation (Romans 8:1).
8: Rahab's pre-sleep declaration to the spies (Joshua 2:9-11) is among the great confessions of faith in the OT — made before any visible result, based entirely on what she had heard (shama'nu, 'we have heard'). This faith pattern is paradigmatic: 'So faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ' (Romans 10:17). Rahab believed on the basis of testimony about YHWH's acts — the Red Sea, Sihon, Og — without having witnessed any of them. She types the Gentile believer who hears the gospel, receives it by faith, and acts on it before the visible conquest arrives. Hebrews 11:31 identifies her explicitly as one who acted by faith.
9: 'I know that the LORD has given you the land' — Rahab's confession is a perfect expression of the faith that grasps divine promise as done before it is historically visible. 'Faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen' (Hebrews 11:1). Rahab's 'I know' (yada'ti) reflects the same certainty that Paul expresses: 'I know whom I have believed, and I am convinced that he is able to guard until that day what has been entrusted to me' (2 Timothy 1:12). The Gentile prostitute models the NT's definition of saving faith — certainty based not on present evidence but on the word of a faithful God.
10: Rahab's specific knowledge of YHWH's acts — the parting of the Red Sea and the defeat of Sihon and Og — is 'the word about Christ' before Christ came (1 Peter 1:10-12). The prophets who spoke of grace to come 'searched and inquired carefully, inquiring what person or time the Spirit of Christ in them was indicating when he predicted the sufferings of Christ and the subsequent glories.' Rahab heard the reports of YHWH's mighty acts and drew the correct theological conclusion: this God is the real God, and he is coming to claim his land. The NT equivalent is hearing the word of the cross and resurrection and drawing the same conclusion: this God is real, and he is coming to claim his world.
11: 'For the LORD your God, he is God in the heavens above and on the earth beneath' — Rahab's confession is monotheistic in form and function. She renounces the entire Canaanite pantheon in a single declaration, naming YHWH as the only real God. This confession parallels the NT's repeated assertion that Christ is Lord over all competing powers: 'that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth' (Philippians 2:10). Rahab's conversion is the advance guard of the Gentile mission: before a single city fell, a Gentile had already confessed YHWH as the only God and transferred her allegiance. She is Abraham's spiritual daughter before she becomes Christ's biological ancestor.
12: Rahab's request for hesed (covenant loyalty, faithful love) in exchange for her loyalty is the earliest picture of Gentile inclusion in the covenant of grace. She asks to be included in the covenant community on the basis of her demonstrated faith — and is granted it. The NT's inclusion of Rahab in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:5) and in the hall of faith (Hebrews 11:31) confirms that her covenant status was genuine. She types every Gentile who receives covenant inclusion through faith in Christ: 'Remember that you were at that time separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel... but now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ' (Ephesians 2:12-13).
13: Rahab's plea for the lives of 'my father and mother, my brothers and sisters, and all who belong to them' — the whole household — types the household-salvation pattern of the NT. Cornelius's household, the Philippian jailer's household, Lydia's household — the NT's salvation events consistently extend to the whole family unit (Acts 10:2; Joshua 16:31-34; Joshua 16:15). The promise 'believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household' (Acts 16:31) echoes the Rahab type: covenant salvation extends through the believing member to the whole household connected to them. The house marked by the scarlet cord was the unit of salvation.
14: 'Our life for yours even to death' — the spies' oath to Rahab prefigures Christ's own oath to those who trust him. He stakes his life for theirs — literally. 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13). The conditional protection the spies offered ('if you do not tell this business of ours') is replaced in Christ by unconditional covenant faithfulness — 'nothing will separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 8:38-39). The spies could only promise protection contingent on Rahab's continued silence; Christ's protection is unconditional, resting on his own righteousness rather than on the protected one's performance.
15: Rahab's letting the spies down through her window by a rope types the 'rope' connections that run through salvation history — Paul was let down through a window in a basket at Damascus to escape arrest (2 Corinthians 11:33), echoing the Rahab escape. More theologically, the 'way out' from the doomed city through the window in the wall types Christ as the escape route through the barrier of death: 'I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved' (John 10:9). Rahab's house in the wall provided the one opening through which the condemned could escape judgment — Christ is the one opening through which humanity escapes the condemned creation.
16: The three days the spies hid in the hill country before returning to Joshua carries the resurrection pattern: three days of concealment followed by return and report. Jonah's three days in the whale, Isaac's three-day journey to Moriah, and these three days of the spies' hiding all participate in the pattern that Jesus identified as his own sign: 'For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matthew 12:40). The hidden-alive-for-three-days pattern is YHWH's signature on the resurrection narrative before it occurs historically.
17: The conditions of the spies' oath — the scarlet thread in the window, the family gathered inside, silence maintained — type the conditions of salvation in Christ. The one condition is not works but identification with the covenant sign: those who are 'inside' (in Christ, marked with his blood-sign) are safe; those who go outside are exposed to judgment. The parallel to Passover (blood on the doorposts, remaining inside the house, Exodus 12:22-23) is deliberate: two covenant-blood-marking events frame the exodus and the conquest, both requiring the same response — stay under the blood, remain inside. Christ is the one in whom the saved remain (John 15:4-5).
19: 'His blood shall be on his own head' — the blood-responsibility formula here is the mirror image of the crowd's declaration at Jesus's trial: 'His blood be on us and on our children!' (Matthew 27:25). The Jericho judgment fell on those who rejected the sign and went outside Rahab's house; the gospel's blood-responsibility falls on those who reject Christ's blood as the covenant sign. The protection Rahab's household received by staying under the scarlet sign types the protection believers receive by remaining in Christ — those who 'go out' from the community of faith (1 John 2:19) put themselves outside the zone of covenant protection.
20: The silence required of Rahab — 'if you tell this business of ours' — has a parallel in the so-called 'messianic secret' of Mark's Gospel: Jesus repeatedly commands silence about his identity during the conquest phase of his ministry (Mark 1:44; Joshua 3:12; Joshua 5:43; Joshua 7:36; Joshua 8:30). The timing of revelation matters in YHWH's warfare: premature disclosure disrupts the plan. Rahab's maintained silence protected the spies' mission; the disciples' maintained silence (until the resurrection) protected the unfolding of the conquest of death. In both cases, the appointed time for full disclosure comes after the victory is accomplished.
21: Rahab's tying of the scarlet cord 'in the window' is the act of covenant ratification — she identified her house as belonging to YHWH's side before the battle was fought. This is baptism in its functional logic: 'In him also you were circumcised with a circumcision made without hands... having been buried with him in baptism, in which you were also raised with him through faith' (Colossians 2:11-12). The external sign of the scarlet cord publicly marked Rahab's transfer of allegiance before any visible rescue occurred. Faith marks itself externally — the cord in the window, the baptismal waters, the Lord's Supper — as a visible declaration of covenant identity under the blood.
22: The three-day hiding in the hill country, away from the Jordan, before the return to Joshua — three days in a kind of death-hiding, three days before the return to the commander who will bring life — types the three days of Christ's burial. The disciples scattered to the hills (Mark 14:50) and hid behind locked doors (John 20:19) in exactly the same way: three days of terrified hiding before the return of the one who brings life. The spies returned and reported 'Truly the LORD has given all the land into our hands' — the disciples returned and reported 'He has risen' (Matthew 28:8). Both reports are the same theological announcement: YHWH has already given the victory.
23: The spies' return and report — 'Truly the LORD has given all the land into our hands, and also all the inhabitants of the land melt away because of us' — is a resurrection-announcement pattern. The risen Christ's own words echo this: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18). Both announcements use the perfect/accomplished tense: the victory is already given, already achieved, even though the visible conquest has not yet been completed. The Christological pattern is 'already-not-yet': the land is given (the resurrection has happened), but the conquest of each city still lies ahead (the sanctification and final parousia are still in process).
24: 'Truly the LORD has given all the land into our hands, and also all the inhabitants of the land melt away because of us' — the spies' report is the conquest's prologue and promise. This is the hermeneutical key to the entire book: what YHWH has declared accomplished by promise, Israel must now actualize by faith. The NT applies the same logic to Christ's victory over sin and death: 'The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet' (Romans 16:20) — the victory is Christ's (Romans 16:20a, 'The God of peace will crush Satan'), but it is actualized in the believer's life ('under your feet'). The spies' confident report types the apostolic proclamation: the victory is given; go and take possession.
